text: William James, writing in 1890, described voluntary attention as a resource that 'comes in beats' — brief flickers of focused direction rather than a sustained beam. He argued that what we call 'sustained attention' is really rapid reorientation: the will repeatedly recaptures the same object. This insight anticipates modern findings on attention blink and microsaccades by over a century. Timed focus sprints operationalize James's observation: rather than demanding that attention hold without wavering, a sprint asks only that you keep returning to the task within a bounded window. The discipline is not in never drifting; it is in noticing the drift and returning before the sprint clock expires.
question: According to the passage, what did William James argue 'sustained attention' actually consists of?

options.text: A slow expansion of working memory capacity through deliberate practice
Suppression of the default mode network through acts of conscious willpower
A neurological state achievable only after twenty minutes of undisturbed work
Repeated rapid reorientations back to the same object rather than a continuous beam
